docs.title: Rene Peuser
docs.text:
• :material-account-hard-hat:{ .lg .middle } Principal Software Architect

    More than two decades of software engineering, from industrial automation and HMIs to enterprise cloud platforms and framework engineering.

  • :material-hammer-wrench:{ .lg .middle } Framework & SDK Engineer

    Reusable .NET foundations, Minimal API platforms, validation engines, OpenAPI tooling, test SDKs, and developer automation.

  • :material-package-variant:{ .lg .middle } Open-Source Maintainer

    Nine packages and more than 4.1 million cumulative downloads on the public NuGet profile as of July 2026.

  • :material-presentation:{ .lg .middle } Conference Speaker

    Speaker at BASTA! and an experienced trainer for architecture, testing, WPF, MVVM, clean code, and modern .NET.

> whoami

Name............... Rene Peuser
Role............... Principal Software Architect
Speciality......... Framework and platform engineering
Mission............ Make product teams faster through reusable foundations
Current focus...... Enterprise AI platform infrastructure
Primary stack...... C# · .NET · ASP.NET Core · Minimal APIs
Passion............. Testing · OpenAPI · Developer Experience · Automation

docs.title: I build engineering multipliers
docs.text:
A well-designed framework does more than remove duplicate code. It creates consistent APIs, centralizes cross-cutting concerns, raises the quality floor, and turns architectural knowledge into executable software.

flowchart LR
    Domain[Product domain] --> Platform[Reusable engineering platform]
    Platform --> API[Minimal APIs]
    Platform --> Validation[Validation]
    Platform --> OpenAPI[OpenAPI]
    Platform --> Testing[Testing SDK]
    Platform --> Observability[Observability]
    Platform --> Deploy[Deployment]

docs.title: Capability meta-framework
docs.text:
A metadata-driven engine for representing and exposing heterogeneous cloud capabilities through consistent contracts, validation, OpenAPI schemas, lifecycle behavior, and provider abstractions.

Read the case study →

docs.title: Enterprise Minimal API platform
docs.text:
A reusable platform for designing, validating, documenting, testing, and operating a large API surface without repeating infrastructure concerns in every endpoint.

Read the case study →

docs.title: AspNetCore.Simple.MsTest.Sdk
docs.text:
A public test SDK built to make request/response integration tests, snapshots, schema assertions, content checks, and API regression tests concise and maintainable.

Read the case study →

docs.title: Engineering philosophy
docs.text:
Build once. Reuse everywhere. Automate everything.

I believe infrastructure concerns such as versioning, error handling, validation, OpenAPI, testing, authentication, and observability should be solved deliberately, packaged cleanly, and reused consistently. Product teams should spend their energy on the domain—not on rebuilding plumbing.

Explore the philosophy →

docs.title: Build once
docs.text:
Repeated infrastructure code is not merely an inconvenience. It creates inconsistent behavior, fragmented maintenance, and uneven quality across teams.

When a concern is stable, cross-cutting, and repeated, I prefer to solve it deliberately and expose it through a reusable abstraction.

docs.title: Reuse everywhere
docs.text:
Reuse is valuable only when the abstraction is understandable, predictable, and easier to adopt than to bypass.

A successful internal platform should provide:

  • strong defaults;
  • explicit extension points;
  • diagnostics that explain failures;
  • composable components;
  • compatibility and migration strategies;
  • documentation backed by executable examples.

docs.title: Automate everything
docs.text: Humans are excellent at judgment and poor at repeating mechanical checks forever. Build pipelines, analyzers, tests, generators, and conventions should protect architecture continuously.

docs.title: Product teams should own the domain
docs.text:
Developers should not repeatedly spend time wiring:

  • API versioning;
  • Problem Details;
  • OpenAPI metadata;
  • authentication scaffolding;
  • validation dispatch;
  • health checks;
  • test hosts;
  • serialization defaults;
  • logging and observability.

A platform team can solve these once and let product teams focus on business value.

docs.title: Testing is not an afterthought
docs.text: Testability is one of the clearest indicators of architectural quality. If a framework is difficult to test, it is usually difficult to understand, extend, or operate.

docs.title: Developer experience is architecture
docs.text: An API that is technically powerful but confusing in daily use has failed. Naming, discoverability, error messages, examples, and tooling are architectural concerns because they directly affect how a system evolves.

docs.title: Engineering principles
docs.text:
1. Prefer explicit intent over clever code.
  2. Turn repeated infrastructure into reusable platform capabilities.
  3. Make the correct path the easiest path.
  4. Design APIs for discoverability, consistency, and migration.
  5. Treat testing, diagnostics, and documentation as product features.
  6. Automate architectural rules where possible.
  7. Measure performance before optimizing—but design with cost awareness.
  8. Use conventions to remove noise, not to hide behavior.
  9. Keep domain logic independent from infrastructure mechanics.
  10. Make extension points intentional rather than accidental.
  11. Avoid abstractions that merely rename complexity.
  12. Continuously remove boilerplate from product teams.

docs.title: Executive profile
docs.text:
I am a software architect, consultant, framework engineer, and open-source maintainer with more than 20 years of professional experience.

My career began in industrial automation, machine commissioning, PLC systems, servo drives, and VB6 user interfaces. It evolved through WPF, MVVM, custom controls, Visual Studio extensions, Roslyn analysis, ASP.NET Core, distributed systems, cloud platforms, and finally large reusable engineering ecosystems.

Today, my work is centered on a simple question:

How can one architectural investment make many engineers and many products better?

That question has led me to build:

  • reusable ASP.NET Core and Minimal API foundations;
  • testing SDKs and domain-specific test APIs;
  • metadata-driven capability systems;
  • cross-property and conditional validation engines;
  • OpenAPI schema and polymorphism infrastructure;
  • reusable error-handling and Problem Details components;
  • cloud provider abstractions and deployment orchestration;
  • Roslyn-based quality controls, CLI tooling, templates, and automation.

docs.title: Professional positioning
docs.text:
Primary roles

  • Principal Software Architect
  • Principal / Staff Software Engineer
  • Framework Engineer
  • Platform Engineer
  • SDK Author
  • Developer Experience Engineer
  • Technical Consultant

docs.title: What differentiates me
docs.text:
I do not treat frameworks as collections of helpers. A framework is an executable architecture:

  • its API design communicates intent;
  • its defaults encode best practices;
  • its extension points determine longevity;
  • its diagnostics determine operability;
  • its testability determines confidence;
  • its documentation determines adoption.

My strongest work usually appears where architecture, developer experience, runtime behavior, and testing meet.

docs.title: Recurring themes throughout my career
docs.text:
• Recognizing patterns and extracting reusable systems
  • Building tools that make other developers faster
  • Strongly typed, discoverable APIs
  • Automated verification over manual process
  • Performance-conscious asynchronous and parallel execution
  • Architecture expressed through code, tests, analyzers, and templates
  • Deep integration of documentation and testability

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
• Names should reveal semantics.
  • Similar concepts should use consistent signatures.
  • Defaults should be safe and predictable.
  • Optionality should be explicit.
  • Public contracts should not expose internal mechanics.
  • Extension points should be narrow and deliberate.
  • Error behavior is part of the API.
  • OpenAPI output is part of the API.
  • Migration paths matter as much as initial elegance.

docs.title: Attribute API example
docs.text:
Prefer:

[RequiredWhen(
    nameof(JobType),
    ConditionOperator.NotEqualsTo,
    JobType.PythonShell)]

over an attribute that exposes implementation-oriented delegates or opaque condition objects.

The semantic form remains readable in domain models and can be interpreted by validation, OpenAPI, samples, and tooling.

docs.title: Framework engineering
docs.text: A framework is successful when it improves the economics and quality of many products.

docs.title: When to extract a framework
docs.text:
A repeated implementation is a candidate when:

  • the concern appears across multiple services or teams;
  • behavior should be consistent;
  • changes should propagate centrally;
  • the abstraction has a stable conceptual core;
  • adoption can be easier than custom implementation.

docs.title: Risks
docs.text:
• premature abstraction;
  • hidden behavior;
  • excessive reflection;
  • generic APIs that erase semantics;
  • forced uniformity;
  • migration complexity;
  • insufficient diagnostics.

docs.title: Countermeasures
docs.text:
• start from real use cases;
  • retain typed semantic APIs;
  • keep extension points explicit;
  • provide golden-path examples;
  • invest in tests and compatibility;
  • treat documentation as part of the API;
  • measure adoption friction.

docs.title: Experience
docs.text:
• asynchronous and parallel execution;
  • TPL and PLINQ;
  • scale-up optimization as preparation for scale-out;
  • memory and CPU profiling;
  • startup and reflection-cost reduction;
  • caching of metadata and type discovery;
  • test-suite initialization and fixture optimization;
  • build resource and packaging optimization;
  • AWS Lambda and SnapStart considerations.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Performance work begins with measurement. However, architecture should avoid known structural costs such as repeated reflection scans, unnecessary allocations, serial I/O, and duplicated initialization.

docs.title: Tooling history
docs.text: Visual Studio profiling tools, Redgate ANTS Performance Profiler, Redgate ANTS Memory Profiler, load testing, application telemetry, and targeted benchmarking.

docs.title: Testing is architecture
docs.text:
Testing reveals the shape of a system.

A design that is easy to exercise through stable public behavior is usually better separated and more understandable than one requiring extensive internal knowledge.

docs.title: Testing layers
docs.text:
• Unit tests for focused rules
  • Contract tests for public schemas and protocols
  • Integration tests through real ASP.NET Core hosts
  • Snapshot tests for broad regression detection
  • Architecture tests for solution and dependency constraints
  • Generated samples to verify documentation and contracts

docs.title: Framework responsibility
docs.text: A reusable platform should ship testing affordances alongside runtime features. Product teams should not reverse-engineer internal infrastructure merely to verify their endpoints.

docs.title: Architecture Decision Records
docs.text:
ADR Status Topic ADR-001 Proposed / evolving Conditional property validation ADR-002 Open Validator naming and synchronization across infrastructure and backend repositories

These ADRs are public, generalized versions of engineering discussions. They intentionally omit confidential repository and domain details.

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Proposed / evolving

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Contracts require semantic rules such as:

  • a property must be null when another property has a value;
  • a property is required unless a particular mode is selected;
  • two properties must differ;
  • a value must satisfy a constraint only under a source-property condition.

C# attributes cannot naturally accept runtime delegates or expression trees because attribute arguments are restricted to compile-time representable values.

docs.title: Decision direction
docs.text:
Use a reusable conditional-validation base that accepts:

  • source property name;
  • condition operator;
  • optional comparison value;
  • optional sample values.

Derived semantic attributes implement the target constraint.

public sealed class MustBeNullWhenAttribute(...) 
    : ConditionalPropertyValidationAttribute(...)
{
    internal override bool SatisfiesConstraint(
        ConditionalPropertyValidationContext context)
        => context.TargetValue is null;
}

docs.title: Positive
docs.text:
• Readable domain contracts
  • Reusable condition evaluation
  • Shared diagnostics
  • OpenAPI and sample integration
  • Semantic derived attributes

docs.title: Negative
docs.text:
• Property names use nameof rather than strongly typed expressions
  • Reflection is required
  • Comparison-value conversion requires careful diagnostics
  • Operator and attribute vocabulary must remain stable

docs.title: Status
docs.text: Open for team decision

docs.title: Context
docs.text: Validation rules exist in an infrastructure repository and in a backend validation engine. Names and concepts may match, differ, or exist on only one side.

docs.title: A. Maintain an explicit mapping table
docs.text:
Keep the backend vocabulary and map infrastructure names to backend validators.

Advantages

  • Avoids unnecessary renaming
  • Preserves backend semantics
  • Makes compatibility explicit

Disadvantages

  • Permanent mapping maintenance
  • More cognitive overhead
  • Potential drift

docs.title: B. Adopt infrastructure validator names in the backend
docs.text:
Align names directly.

Advantages

  • Simple cross-repository comparison
  • Reduced mapping logic

Disadvantages

  • Backend API churn
  • Infrastructure names may not be ideal semantic APIs
  • Migration and compatibility costs

docs.title: C. Define a canonical validation vocabulary
docs.text:
Create a versioned canonical rule catalogue and map both implementations to it.

Advantages

  • Clear long-term ownership
  • Separates concepts from implementation names
  • Supports governance and tooling

Disadvantages

  • Highest initial effort
  • Requires team agreement and migration planning

docs.title: Decision criteria
docs.text:
• API clarity
  • backward compatibility
  • code-generation requirements
  • OpenAPI representation
  • governance ownership
  • migration cost
  • frequency of rule changes

docs.title: Siemens
docs.text:
Period: 2020–present Positioning: Principal backend / platform engineer and technical consultant

This page intentionally stays at a public, non-confidential abstraction level.

docs.title: Mission
docs.text: Design and evolve reusable backend foundations for a large enterprise cloud and AI ecosystem.

docs.title: Areas of responsibility
docs.text:
• Architecture and implementation of reusable ASP.NET Core and Minimal API SDKs
  • Metadata-driven capability modeling
  • Multi-provider abstractions across AWS, Azure, GCP, OpenAI, Snowflake, Fabric, and internal platforms
  • OpenAPI generation and schema transformation
  • Validation engines and reusable validation rules
  • Test infrastructure and integration-testing SDKs
  • Error-handling and Problem Details infrastructure
  • Deployment lifecycle modeling and orchestration integration
  • Performance, reflection, startup, and build optimization
  • Pair programming, code reviews, documentation, and technical enablement

docs.title: Large Minimal API ecosystem
docs.text: Contributed to a platform with hundreds of endpoints and a broad capability model. The engineering challenge is not simply endpoint implementation; it is maintaining consistency across contracts, validation, schemas, provider behavior, lifecycle operations, testing, and generated clients.

docs.title: Capability meta-framework
docs.text: Designed and evolved a framework in which capability types can be represented through metadata and conventions. This makes it possible to introduce new capability families without rebuilding every infrastructural concern independently.

docs.title: OpenAPI and request polymorphism
docs.text: Implemented and refined OpenAPI support for typed create requests, discriminators, oneOf schemas, provider mappings, duplicate prevention, and self-reference handling.

docs.title: Validation platform
docs.text: Built and expanded reusable rules for strings, collections, identifiers, property relationships, conditional requirements, numeric limits, and domain-specific constraints. Work includes aligning rule vocabularies across infrastructure and backend repositories.

docs.title: Testing platform
docs.text: Developed abstractions for API integration testing, response assertions, schemas, snapshots, content types, status codes, request samples, and reusable test clients.

docs.title: Cloud and deployment model
docs.text: Worked on capability and deployment behavior spanning AWS, Azure, GCP, Snowflake, Fabric, OpenAI, and internal platforms. Relevant services include AWS Lambda, DynamoDB, Cognito, API Gateway, Step Functions, SSM Parameter Store, and cloud-specific AI services.

docs.title: Engineering style
docs.text:
I combine implementation with architecture enablement:

  • design the reusable core;
  • provide strong examples and tests;
  • remove accidental complexity;
  • coach teams through reviews and pair programming;
  • encode conventions in executable tooling.

docs.title: Scope

  • Coaching around API development, testing, and clean code

docs.title: Technologies

docs.title: 1998–2007 · Industrial systems
docs.text:
My engineering foundation was formed close to machines: industrial electronics, PLC programming, servo drives, process control, user interfaces, commissioning, troubleshooting, and customer training.

This environment taught me that software quality is operational reality. A defect is not an abstract ticket; it can stop an entire machine.

docs.title: 2008–2014 · C#, WPF, and UI architecture
docs.text: I moved deeply into C#, WPF, MVVM, custom controls, scalable UI architectures, asynchronous execution, and developer tooling. I built CAD-like interaction systems and Visual Studio extensions to automate localization and repetitive development work.

docs.title: 2015–2020 · Architecture automation
docs.text:
At STOLL and TRUMPF, my work increasingly focused on:

  • scalable and testable code;
  • automated view tests;
  • Roslyn-based architectural checks;
  • solution and project configuration tests;
  • performance and memory analysis;
  • reusable object creation and test infrastructure;
  • Visual Studio extensions and .NET global tools.

docs.title: 2020–today · Backend platforms and framework engineering
docs.text:
The focus shifted toward ASP.NET Core, APIs, cloud infrastructure, reusable SDKs, test platforms, OpenAPI, validation, deployment abstractions, and metadata-driven architecture.

The progression is consistent: from building individual systems to building foundations used by many systems.

docs.title: Focus
docs.text: Industrial HMI systems, WPF architecture, automated testing, developer tooling, performance engineering, and gradual adoption of ASP.NET Core.

  • Code reviews, refactoring, and testability improvements

docs.text: The test SDK targets concise, file-backed, request/response-oriented API tests. Its public package metadata emphasizes API testing, snapshot testing, integration testing, contract testing, HTTP assertions, JSON comparison, and regression testing.

docs.title: Source and confidentiality policy
docs.text:
This portfolio combines:

  • public package metadata;
  • public conference and repository links;
  • historical information from my resume;
  • generalized descriptions of professional work.

docs.title: Confidentiality
docs.text:
Employer and customer work is described only at an architectural level. This repository must not contain:

  • internal URLs or repository names;
  • credentials or identifiers;
  • customer data;
  • proprietary algorithms;
  • non-public source code;
  • internal diagrams;
  • confidential performance, cost, or business metrics.

docs.title: Statistics
docs.text: Package counts, versions, and downloads are time-sensitive. Every statistic should include a date and link to the live source.

docs.title: Accuracy
docs.text: Unverified claims should be marked TODO: verify rather than presented as fact.

docs.title: Project portfolio
docs.text:
The projects in this portfolio are selected because they demonstrate reusable engineering rather than isolated features.

Project Primary engineering theme Capability meta-framework Metadata, extensibility, provider abstraction Minimal API platform Reusable API infrastructure Testing SDK Developer experience and regression safety Validation engine Semantic rules and cross-property constraints OpenAPI infrastructure Schemas, polymorphism, generated contracts Error handling Consistent Problem Details and diagnostics Deployment/provider model Multi-cloud lifecycle abstraction Developer experience CLI, analyzers, templates, automation

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
A multi-cloud platform can contain many heterogeneous capability types: storage, compute, databases, AI services, networking, integrations, and provider-specific resources.

Implementing every type as an isolated vertical slice leads to duplicated infrastructure and inconsistent behavior.

docs.title: Architectural idea
docs.text:
Represent stable capability characteristics through metadata and conventions, while retaining typed contracts for domain-specific behavior.

flowchart TB
    Meta[Capability metadata] --> Discovery[Type discovery]
    Meta --> Schema[OpenAPI schema]
    Meta --> Validation[Validation rules]
    Meta --> Routing[Endpoint routing]
    Meta --> Samples[Samples and tests]
    Discovery --> Providers[Provider implementations]
    Providers --> AWS
    Providers --> Azure
    Providers --> GCP
    Providers --> Other[Other platforms]

docs.title: Responsibilities
docs.text:
The framework coordinates:

  • capability type discovery;
  • typed create and update contracts;
  • provider and capability mappings;
  • generic endpoint behavior;
  • validation metadata;
  • OpenAPI schemas and discriminators;
  • relationship modeling;
  • deployment metadata;
  • generated samples and integration tests.

docs.title: Design goals
docs.text:
• Add new capability types with minimal infrastructural duplication.
  • Keep contracts strongly typed and discoverable.
  • Preserve provider-specific semantics without leaking them everywhere.
  • Generate consistent OpenAPI and client-facing models.
  • Make validation rules reusable and centrally testable.
  • Support hundreds of endpoints without hundreds of bespoke implementations.

docs.title: Key challenge
docs.text: A meta-framework must avoid becoming a “magic” system. The architecture therefore favors explicit metadata, predictable conventions, diagnostics, and escape hatches over hidden runtime behavior.

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text: The result is an engineering multiplier: new capabilities reuse platform behavior instead of reimplementing routing, validation, schemas, testing, and lifecycle mechanics.

docs.title: Context
docs.text: A capability platform spans heterogeneous providers and deployment lifecycles. AWS Lambda, GCP Vertex AI, Snowflake, Fabric, and other services do not expose identical resource models.

docs.title: Approach
docs.text:
Use a common lifecycle model for platform concerns while preserving provider-specific implementations.

flowchart TB
    API[Capability API] --> Model[Common deployment model]
    Model --> Orchestrator[Orchestration]
    Orchestrator --> AWS[AWS adapter]
    Orchestrator --> Azure[Azure adapter]
    Orchestrator --> GCP[GCP adapter]
    Orchestrator --> Data[Data/AI platforms]

docs.title: Areas addressed
docs.text:
• queued, in-progress, success, failure, and deletion states;
  • idempotency and valid state transitions;
  • deployment metadata;
  • provider-specific orchestration;
  • relation types between capabilities;
  • cleanup behavior;
  • stage-aware persistence;
  • generic and provider-specific validation.

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: Normalize what is genuinely common. Do not erase important provider semantics merely to force a uniform interface.

docs.title: Developer experience
docs.text: Developer experience is where architecture becomes daily reality.

docs.title: Tooling themes
docs.text:
• NuGet-based reusable SDKs
  • .NET global tools
  • CLI filtering and automation
  • Visual Studio extensions
  • Roslyn analyzers
  • templates and project foundations
  • code-generation and schema-generation workflows
  • test clients and assertion DSLs
  • technical documentation and examples
  • build optimization and pipeline checks

docs.title: Historical examples
docs.text:
• Visual Studio extensions that generated WPF view tests
  • localization tooling for RESX and ResourceDictionary workflows
  • Roslyn-based architecture rules
  • solution and project setting verification
  • CLI tools for repetitive engineering tasks
  • reusable API and test templates

docs.title: Design test
docs.text: A platform feature is incomplete until a developer can discover it, adopt it, diagnose it, test it, and migrate it.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Provide consistent, structured, observable error contracts across services.

docs.title: Core concepts
docs.text:
• RFC 7807 / Problem Details responses
  • Central exception mapping
  • Consistent status-code selection
  • Correlation and contextual metadata
  • Logging integration
  • Sanitized client-facing details
  • Extensible domain-specific problem information
  • Testable error behavior

docs.title: Public package
docs.text: Siemens.AspNet.ErrorHandling documents the use of RFC 7807 to provide consistent and interpretable error responses.

docs.title: Architectural benefit
docs.text: Centralized error handling separates endpoint code from protocol mechanics and ensures that observability, client contracts, and security rules evolve consistently.

docs.title: Objective
docs.text: Provide a production-ready foundation for large ASP.NET Core Minimal API systems.

docs.title: Platform capabilities
docs.text:
• Endpoint and contract conventions
  • API versioning
  • OpenAPI document and schema transformation
  • Authentication and authorization integration
  • Validation dispatch and reusable rules
  • Error handling and Problem Details
  • Request/response samples
  • Health checks and diagnostics
  • Logging and observability integration
  • Test-host and client integration
  • Cloud hosting support, including AWS Lambda scenarios

docs.title: Why a platform?
docs.text:
Minimal APIs deliberately expose a small programming model. Enterprise systems still need strong conventions and reusable infrastructure.

Without a platform, each endpoint or service can make different decisions about:

  • route design;
  • validation;
  • status codes;
  • error contracts;
  • versioning;
  • schema generation;
  • logging;
  • testing.

The platform turns those decisions into a consistent engineering system.

docs.title: Scale
docs.text: The current work supports a broad platform with roughly 450 endpoints and multiple cloud providers. The challenge is sustained consistency, not merely endpoint count.

docs.title: Public package
docs.text: Siemens.AspNet.MinimalApi.Sdk exposes part of this engineering direction publicly through NuGet.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: OpenAPI is treated as a first-class contract rather than an afterthought.

docs.title: Selected work
docs.text:
• Typed create-request hierarchies
  • oneOf schema generation
  • Discriminator property and mapping
  • Capability-enum-to-request-type mapping
  • Document transformers
  • Duplicate schema elimination
  • Self-reference protection
  • Provider-aware schema composition
  • Request and response sample integration
  • Generated-client compatibility
  • OpenAPI v1 contract evolution

docs.title: Example model
docs.text:
classDiagram
    CreateCapabilityRequestBase <|-- CreateAwsGlueJobRequest
    CreateCapabilityRequestBase <|-- CreateS3BucketRequest
    CreateCapabilityRequestBase <|-- CreateGcpVertexAiRequest
    CreateCapabilityRequestBase : +CapabilityType Type

docs.title: Principle
docs.text: The schema should accurately express the runtime contract. When polymorphism exists in the API, it should be represented explicitly enough for client generators, documentation tools, validators, and human readers.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
AspNetCore.Simple.MsTest.Sdk reduces boilerplate in ASP.NET Core API tests and makes request/response regression tests readable, repeatable, and maintainable.

View on NuGet

docs.title: Core capabilities
docs.text:
• ASP.NET Core integration-test hosting
  • HTTP request helpers
  • Expected status-code validation
  • JSON response comparison
  • Snapshot and approval-style testing
  • Schema validation
  • Content-type assertions
  • Response-type assertions
  • Reusable request and response files
  • Filtering and tooling for large endpoint suites
  • Structured assertion output designed to be understandable by humans and automation

docs.title: Design goal
docs.text:
A test should communicate the scenario, input, expected response, and expected protocol behavior without pages of setup code.

await Client.AssertPostAsync<CreateRequest>(
    "/api/v1/capabilities",
    "Requests/Create.json",
    "Responses/Create.json",
    expectedStatusCode: HttpStatusCode.Created);

docs.title: Why build a custom SDK?
docs.text:
Large API suites repeat the same mechanics:

  1. construct a test host;
  2. create an HTTP request;
  3. load test data;
  4. send the request;
  5. validate the status code;
  6. normalize and compare JSON;
  7. assert headers and schemas;
  8. generate useful diagnostics.

Centralizing this flow improves consistency, readability, and failure analysis.

docs.title: Public impact
docs.text: As of July 2026, the NuGet package has exceeded 700,000 cumulative downloads. Package statistics change continuously; use the live NuGet page for current figures.

docs.title: Motivation
docs.text:
Enterprise validation extends far beyond [Required].

Real contracts contain:

  • conditional requirements;
  • cross-property comparisons;
  • mutually exclusive values;
  • property-dependent nullability;
  • collection uniqueness and item constraints;
  • domain-specific identifiers;
  • provider-specific limits;
  • relationship rules.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart LR
    Attribute[Semantic attribute] --> Descriptor[Condition descriptor]
    Descriptor --> Evaluator[Condition evaluator]
    Evaluator --> Constraint[Target constraint]
    Constraint --> Result[Validation result]
    Descriptor --> OpenAPI[Schema/sample metadata]

docs.title: Design direction
docs.text:
Semantic attributes express intent:

[MustBeNullWhen(
    nameof(JobType),
    ConditionOperator.EqualsTo,
    AwsGlueJobType.PythonShell)]
public double? WorkerCount { get; init; }

A shared conditional-validation foundation handles:

  • source-property lookup;
  • comparison-value conversion;
  • operator evaluation;
  • target-property constraint evaluation;
  • error-message construction;
  • sample generation;
  • OpenAPI integration.

docs.title: Representative rule families
docs.text:
• required / empty / null conditions;
  • equality and property comparison;
  • starts-with / ends-with;
  • min/max item counts;
  • duplicate detection;
  • numeric ranges;
  • identifier formats;
  • conditional collection rules;
  • provider and capability-specific constraints.

docs.title: Engineering concerns
docs.text:
• Attribute arguments must be compile-time constants.
  • Delegates and expression trees cannot be supplied naturally as attribute constructor arguments.
  • Reflection work should be cached.
  • Error messages must identify source, target, operator, and expected behavior.
  • Rule names should be semantically stable across repositories and languages.

• Principal Software Architect
  • Principal / Staff .NET Engineer
  • Platform Engineer
  • Framework and SDK Engineer
  • Backend Architect
  • Developer Experience Engineer
  • Technical Consultant

docs.title: Value proposition
docs.text: I help organizations turn fragmented application infrastructure into a reusable engineering platform.

docs.title: Typical engagement areas
docs.text:
• Standardizing ASP.NET Core and Minimal API services
  • Designing framework and SDK APIs
  • Building enterprise validation systems
  • Creating integration and regression test platforms
  • Improving OpenAPI and generated-client contracts
  • Extracting provider abstractions for multi-cloud systems
  • Automating architecture rules with Roslyn and tests
  • Reducing boilerplate through templates, CLI tools, and NuGet packages
  • Coaching teams through pair programming and code reviews

docs.title: Personal details
docs.text: Name: Rene Peuser Year of birth: 1981 Experience: More than 20 years Languages: German, English (fluent), Hungarian (basic)

docs.title: Professional summary
docs.text:
Principal-level .NET software architect and framework engineer with a career spanning industrial automation, desktop architecture, developer tooling, APIs, cloud systems, and enterprise platforms.

My central strength is identifying repeated engineering patterns and converting them into reusable SDKs, frameworks, test platforms, analyzers, templates, and automation.

docs.title: Short bio
docs.text: Rene Peuser is a Principal Software Architect, framework engineer, open-source maintainer, and conference speaker with more than 20 years of experience. He specializes in reusable .NET platforms, Minimal APIs, testing SDKs, OpenAPI, validation engines, and developer tooling.

docs.title: Long bio
docs.text: Rene Peuser began his career in industrial automation and machine commissioning before moving into C#, WPF, MVVM, Visual Studio extensions, Roslyn, ASP.NET Core, and cloud platform engineering. Today he designs reusable frameworks and SDKs that standardize API infrastructure, testing, validation, OpenAPI, error handling, and multi-provider capability systems. His public NuGet portfolio has accumulated more than four million downloads.

docs.title: Suggested topics
docs.text:
• Building frameworks instead of repeating infrastructure
  • Testing Minimal APIs at scale
  • Designing a metadata-driven capability platform
  • Conditional validation in C# attribute-based models
  • OpenAPI polymorphism without losing maintainability
  • Developer experience as an architectural concern
  • From industrial automation to platform engineering
